agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers use delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance model.","bounded_context":"Delivery Records / Data Retention & Compliance","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"Delivery Record","definition":"The complete data artifact documenting a delivery lifecycle — including proof of delivery, timestamps, recipient confirmation, chain of custody, and associated metadata. This is a legal document, not just operational data."},{"term":"Proof of Delivery (POD)","definition":"Evidence that a shipment was delivered to the correct recipient at a specific time and place. Required for dispute resolution, insurance claims, and regulatory compliance. Often retained for years after delivery."},{"term":"Data Retention Policy","definition":"The rules governing how long specific categories of data are stored, driven by legal requirements, contractual obligations, and business needs — not storage cost considerations."},{"term":"Compliance Reporting","definition":"The process by which B2B customers use platform delivery data to satisfy their own regulatory and audit obligations. Deleting source data breaks this downstream dependency."},{"term":"Chain of Custody","definition":"The documented trail of who handled a shipment at each stage. Critical for liability determination in damage or loss claims, which can surface months or years after delivery."},{"term":"Right to Erasure (GDPR Art. 17)","definition":"Data subject's right to request deletion of personal data, subject to exceptions including legal obligation retention and establishment/defense of legal claims."},{"term":"Limitation Period","definition":"The legal window during which claims can be brought. In the UK this is typically 6 years (Limitation Act 1980); in many EU jurisdictions 3-10 years. Delivery records are evidence in such claims."}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery records that serve as proof of delivery must be retained for at least the applicable statute of limitations period for contract and consumer claims in each operating jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"This is a legal constraint, not a policy choice. UK Limitation Act 1980 sets 6 years for contract claims. EU member states vary (3-10 years). Deleting evidence before the limitation period expires exposes the company to indefensible litigation. No VP can override statute law.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record is deleted before the longest applicable limitation period has expired for its jurisdiction of delivery."},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"Tax-relevant delivery records (commercial invoices, VAT documentation, customs declarations) must be retained for the period required by tax authorities in each jurisdiction.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"EU VAT Directive requires retention of invoices and supporting documents for periods set by member states (typically 7-10 years). HMRC requires 6 years. Premature deletion constitutes destruction of tax records, which carries criminal penalties.","testable_assertion":"No delivery record containing tax-relevant data is deleted before the applicable tax retention period has expired."},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"B2B customer delivery data must be retained for at least the period specified in the customer's service agreement or SLA.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Contractual obligation. Deleting data that customers rely on for their own compliance reporting is a breach of contract. This is not a discretionary policy — it is a binding legal commitment.","testable_assertion":"No B2B customer delivery record is deleted before the contractual retention period specified in that customer's agreement has expired."},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"Delivery records subject to an active dispute, claim, investigation, or legal hold must never be auto-deleted regardless of retention policy.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"Destroying evidence relevant to active or reasonably anticipated litigation constitutes spoliation of evidence. This is a legal obligation that overrides any retention schedule.","testable_assertion":"Any delivery record flagged with an active dispute, claim, or legal hold is excluded from automated deletion and retained until the hold is released."},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"GDPR requires a lawful basis for both retention AND deletion. Records cannot be deleted solely to reduce costs if there is a legal obligation to retain them, and cannot be retained beyond necessity without lawful basis.","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"GDPR Article 5(1)(e) storage limitation principle and Article 17(3)(b) exception for legal obligations. The retention period must be justified by legal basis, not by storage budget.","testable_assertion":"Every retention period is mapped to a documented lawful basis; no deletion or retention occurs without a corresponding legal justification."}],"acceptance_criteria":[],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"A B2B customer requests delivery data from 8 months ago for a tax audit by their local authority. Under the 90-day policy, this data has been deleted.","expected_behavior":"This must never happen. The platform would be in breach of its SLA and the customer would face regulatory penalties. The customer would have grounds for a substantial damages claim."},{"scenario":"A consumer files a claim 4 years after delivery alleging non-delivery. The platform has no proof of delivery because records were deleted after 90 days.","expected_behavior":"The platform cannot defend itself. Under consumer protection law, burden of proof typically falls on the seller/carrier to prove delivery occurred. Without POD records, the platform loses by default."},{"scenario":"HMRC or an EU tax authority audits the company and requests delivery records supporting VAT filings from 3 years ago.","expected_behavior":"Records must be available. Failure to produce them constitutes a tax compliance violation with potential fines, penalties, and criminal liability for officers."},{"scenario":"A delivery record contains personal data and the data subject exercises their right to erasure under GDPR Article 17.","expected_behavior":"Erasure must be evaluated against Article 17(3) exceptions. If a legal retention obligation applies (tax, limitation period), erasure can be lawfully refused for those fields. Personal data not subject to legal retention should be anonymized or deleted."},{"scenario":"Cross-border delivery between two EU jurisdictions with different limitation periods (e.g., 3 years in one, 10 years in the other).","expected_behavior":"The longer applicable retention period must be used, determined by the jurisdiction whose law governs the contract and/or the jurisdiction of delivery."},{"scenario":"A B2B customer churns but their historical delivery data is still within the contractual or legal retention window.","expected_behavior":"Data must be retained for the full legally required period regardless of customer relationship status. Contractual retention obligations survive termination."}],"business_impact":{"financial":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Breach of B2B SLAs triggers contractual penalties and customer churn — B2B logistics customers chose this platform partly for compliance data availability. (2) Inability to defend against consumer non-delivery claims means automatic payouts. (3) Tax compliance violations carry fines of up to 100% of unpaid tax plus interest in some EU jurisdictions. (4) Storage cost savings are trivial compared to a single regulatory fine or lost enterprise customer. A rough estimate: storage costs for 7 years of delivery records might be tens of thousands annually; a single HMRC penalty or lost B2B contract could be hundreds of thousands to millions.","operational":"SEVERE NEGATIVE. (1) Eliminates the platform's ability to support B2B customer compliance workflows. (2) Destroys audit trail needed for internal quality management and dispute resolution. (3) Creates a data governance crisis requiring emergency re-architecture if regulatory action is taken. (4) Any 'fix' after data is deleted is irreversible — deleted data cannot be recovered.","user":"SEVERE NEGATIVE for B2B customers who depend on the platform as a system of record for their logistics compliance. Moderate negative for B2C consumers who may need delivery records for warranty claims, returns outside standard windows, or insurance claims.","risk":"CRITICAL. (1) Regulatory risk: GDPR, VAT Directive, and national tax law violations. (2) Legal risk: inability to defend claims within limitation periods. (3) Contractual risk: breach of B2B SLAs. (4) Reputational risk: being known as a platform that destroys compliance-critical data. (5) Criminal risk: destruction of tax records can carry personal liability for company officers in several EU jurisdictions. This change, if implemented as proposed, would create existential legal exposure.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"B2B Logistics Customers","impact":"Loss of compliance data they contractually depend on. May trigger SLA breach claims and customer churn. Customers may face their own regulatory penalties."},{"group":"B2C Consumers","impact":"Loss of proof of delivery for disputes, warranty claims, and chargebacks. Reduced consumer protection."},{"group":"Finance / Tax Team","impact":"Unable to produce delivery records for tax audits. Personal liability risk for finance officers."},{"group":"Legal / Compliance Team","impact":"Unable to defend litigation claims. Spoliation of evidence risk. Must manage regulatory exposure."},{"group":"Engineering Team","impact":"Framing this as 'no business logic changes' is incorrect. The retention period IS business logic — it encodes legal obligations. This requires legal review, not just an infra ticket."},{"group":"Executive Leadership","impact":"Personal liability exposure for officers if tax record destruction is deemed willful. Board-level risk."},{"group":"Insurance Partners","impact":"Loss of delivery evidence needed for cargo insurance claims, which can be filed years after delivery."}]},"confidence":{"level":45,"reasoning":"Confidence is low because this proposal has a fundamental contradiction: it is framed as 'no business logic changes' but proposes changing a legally-driven retention period that IS business logic. The spec does not mention any legal review, does not acknowledge statutory retention requirements, and does not differentiate between data categories. The proposal as stated is not implementable without violating multiple laws. Additionally, specific retention periods vary by EU member state and would require jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction legal analysis that is beyond this review.","high_confidence_areas":["UK Limitation Act requires 6-year retention for contract claims","EU VAT Directive mandates multi-year retention of tax-relevant records","B2B SLA obligations survive any internal infrastructure decision","90 days is insufficient for any known EU/UK legal retention requirement","Deleted data is unrecoverable — this change is irreversible"],"low_confidence_areas":["Exact retention periods for each EU member state the platform operates in","Specific contractual retention terms in existing B2B agreements","Whether the platform stores tax-relevant data within delivery records or separately","Whether any data anonymization approach could satisfy both GDPR minimization and legal retention"],"assumptions":["Delivery records contain or are linked to proof of delivery, recipient data, and transaction details","B2B customer agreements include data availability or retention commitments","The platform has not already separated tax-relevant data into a separate retention tier","No legal review has been conducted for this proposal"]},"escalations":[{"type":"contradiction","description":"The proposal is framed as 'infrastructure optimization — no business logic changes' but a data retention period for delivery records in EU/UK markets IS business logic. It directly encodes legal obligations under the UK Limitation Act, EU VAT Directive, GDPR, and national commercial codes. Changing it from 7 years to 90 days is not an infrastructure decision — it is a legal and compliance decision that requires legal counsel review before any implementation work begins.","affected_stakeholders":["B2B Logistics Customers","B2C Consumers","Finance/Tax Team","Legal/Compliance Team","Executive Leadership"],"options":["A: Reject the proposal entirely and maintain 7-year retention","B: Engage legal counsel to conduct a jurisdiction-by-jurisdiction retention analysis, then implement tiered retention where different data categories have different retention periods based on legal requirements","C: Implement data lifecycle management that anonymizes personal data after legal minimums while retaining non-personal delivery records for the full required period"],"recommendation":"Option B. The current 7-year policy likely exists for good reason — it covers the UK 6-year limitation period plus margin. Rather than blanket reduction, engage legal counsel to define minimum retention per data category per jurisdiction, then implement intelligent tiered retention. This can still reduce storage costs (e.g., moving older records to cold storage, anonymizing personal data after GDPR minimums) without creating legal exposure. Do NOT proceed with any implementation until legal review is complete."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"Critical information is missing: (1) Which specific EU member states does the platform operate in? Retention requirements vary significantly. (2) What do existing B2B contracts say about data retention/availability? (3) Has legal counsel reviewed this proposal? (4) Are delivery records the system of record for tax-relevant data or is that stored separately? (5) What is the actual storage cost being targeted, and has cold/archival storage been considered as an alternative?","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering Team","Legal/Compliance Team","Executive Leadership"],"options":["Gather missing context before proceeding","Proceed with assumptions"],"recommendation":"Gather all missing context. This proposal cannot be safely evaluated without legal review and contract analysis. The risk of proceeding without this information is existential."}],"rejection_reasons":[{"violation":"Proposal would delete delivery records before statutory limitation periods expire","business_rule_broken":"BR-1: Delivery records must be retained for the applicable statute of limitations (6 years UK, 3-10 years EU)"},{"violation":"Proposal would destroy tax-relevant records before tax authority retention requirements are met","business_rule_broken":"BR-2: Tax-relevant delivery records must be retained for periods required by tax authorities (6-10 years depending on jurisdiction)"},{"violation":"Proposal would breach B2B customer SLAs that depend on delivery data availability for compliance reporting","business_rule_broken":"BR-3: B2B customer data must be retained per contractual obligations"},{"violation":"Proposal is framed as 'no business logic changes' when it fundamentally alters a legally-driven business rule","business_rule_broken":"BR-5: Retention and deletion must be driven by lawful basis, not storage cost optimization"}]}
agent_output_excerpt: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"Proposed reduction of delivery record retention from 7 years to 90 days, framed as infrastructure optimization. The platform operates in EU and UK markets serving both B2C consumers and B2B logistics companies. B2B customers use delivery data for their own compliance reporting. This is NOT an infrastructure optimization — it is a fundamental change to the platform's legal compliance posture, contractual obligations, and data governance model.","bounded_con
